elements.sent-2411-001: Let $\MM$ be the group of multiplicative characters of a finite field $\FF$, and let $\JJ(\alpha, \beta)$ be the Jacobi sum, for $\alpha, \beta \in \MM$.
elements.sent-2411-002: We observe that the function $\JJ \colon \MM \times \MM \to \bC$ satisfies three elementary properties.
elements.sent-2411-003: We show that these properties (very nearly) characterize Jacobi sums: if $M$ is an arbitrary non-trivial finite abelian group and $J \colon M \times M \to \bC$ is a function satisfying these properties then $M$ is naturally the group of multiplicative characters of a finite field and $J$ is the Jacobi sum.
elements.sent-2411-004: Let $\FF$ be a finite field with $q$ elements and let $\MM$ be the group of complex valued characters of the multiplicative group $\FF^*$.
elements.sent-2411-005: For $\alpha, \beta \in \MM$, we define the \defn{Jacobi sum by \begin{displaymath \JJ(\alpha, \beta) = \frac{1 {q-1 \sum_{\substack{x+y=1, x,y \in \FF^* \alpha(x) \beta(y).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Jacobi sums satisfy the following identities (see \S \ref{s:ident ):.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Here $\delta(\alpha)$ is~1 if $\alpha$ is the trivial character, and~0 otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our goal is to show that these properties (very nearly) characterize Jacobi sums.
elements.sent-2411-012: To formulate our result, it will be convenient to introduce the following piece of terminology:.
elements.sent-2411-013: A \defn{Jacobi function on a finite abelian group $M$ is a function \begin{displaymath J \colon M \times M \to \bC \end{displaymath satisfying the analogs of (A), (B), and (C).}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Of course, the main example of a Jacobi function is a Jacobi sum $\JJ$ associated to a finite field; we refer to this as the ``classical case.'' In what follows, we let $\hat{M}$ denote the Pontryagin dual of a finite abelian group $M$, and we write $\alpha(x)$ for the pairing between $\alpha \in M$ and $x \in \hat{M}$.
elements.sent-2411-015: We are now ready to state our main theorem:.
elements.sent-2411-016: Let $M$ be a finite abelian group of order $m \ge 2$, and let $J$ be a Jacobi function on $M$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Then there is a unique field structure on the set $F=\hat{M \sqcup \{0\}$, where~0 is the additive identity and the multiplication extends the group law on $\hat{M}$, such that \begin{displaymath J(\alpha, \beta) = \frac{1 {m \sum_{\substack{x+y=1, x,y \in F \setminus \{0\} \alpha(x) \beta(y).. \end{displaymath.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: We say a little about the proof.
elements.sent-2411-019: Let us first return to the classical case, where $J=\JJ$ is the Jacobi sum for a finite field $\FF$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Let $X \subset (\FF^*)^2$ be the set of pairs $(x,y)$ where $x+y=1$, and identify $\FF^*$ with $\hat{\MM}$.
elements.sent-2411-021: The function $\JJ$ is exactly the Fourier transform of the indicator function of the set $X$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, given $\JJ$, we can ``discover'' $X$ by taking an inverse Fourier transform.
elements.sent-2411-023: With $X$ in hand, it is easy to reconstruct the addition law on $\FF$.
elements.sent-2411-024: This is how the proof works in general: given an arbitrary Jacobi function $J$, we show that its inverse Fourier transform is the indicator function of a set, and use this set to construct the addition law on $F$.
elements.sent-2411-025: We make a number of remarks concerning the theorem.
elements.sent-2411-026: A Jacobi function $J$ is then determined by the single value $a=J(1,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Conditions (A) and (B) are trivial, while (C) amounts to the equation $a^2=a$.
elements.sent-2411-028: We thus find two solutions to the equations, namely, $a=0$ and $a=1$.
elements.sent-2411-029: The $a=0$ case is the Jacobi sum for the finite field $\bF_2$.
elements.sent-2411-030: The $a=1$ case does not come from a finite field structure, but it can be viewed as the ``Jacobi sum'' for the boolean semi-ring.
elements.sent-2411-031: We now explain how these operations work in the classical situation.
elements.sent-2411-032: Thus suppose $M=\MM$ and $J=\JJ$ for some finite field $\FF$ of cardinality $q$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Let $r$ be an integer coprime to $q-1$ so that $\alpha \mapsto \alpha^r$ is an automorphism of $\MM$.
elements.sent-2411-034: We thus have a Jacobi function $\JJ'$ on $\MM$ by $\JJ'(\alpha, \beta)=\JJ(\alpha^r, \beta^r)$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The map $x \mapsto x^r$ is a bijection $\FF \to \FF$ that is compatible with multiplication, and so we can define a new field structure on $\FF$ by conjugating addition by this map.
elements.sent-2411-036: The Jacobi function $\JJ'$ is the classical Jacobi sum with respect to this new field structure.
elements.sent-2411-037: Next, suppose that $\sigma$ is a field automorphism of $\bC$.
elements.sent-2411-038: We thus obtain a new Jacobi function $\JJ''$ on $\MM$ by $\JJ''=\sigma \circ \JJ$.
elements.sent-2411-039: Now, there is then an integer $r$ coprime to $q-1$ such that $\sigma(\zeta)=\zeta^r$ for all $q-1$ roots of unity $\zeta \in \bC$.
elements.sent-2411-040: One easily sees that $\JJ''$ is exactly the Jacobi function $\JJ'$ defined above.
elements.sent-2411-041: Thus it too is the classical Jacobi sum for the new field structure on $\FF$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Is there a real number analog of our theorem? (iii) One can consider Jacobi sums where the finite field $\FF$ is replaced with a finite ring such as $\bZ/p^n \bZ$ (see, e.g., \cite[\S 5]{Nica ), or even a finite semi-ring.}
elements.sent-2411-043: One can then attempt to characterize these.

elements.sent-2411-045: Given a finite abelian group $M$, there is an oligomorphic group $G=G(M)$ that is defined as the symmetry group of an $M$-weighted Cantor set.
elements.sent-2411-046: It turns out that measures for $G(M)$ correspond to Jacobi functions on $M$.
elements.sent-2411-047: This is how we were led to this particular set of conditions.
